title: podman from brew takes precedence over podman from the podman.io installer
body:
### Issue Description

I recently uninstalled podman/qemu from brew, then installed podman using podman.io installer, and finally installed mongodb atlas through brew.
I did not notice this dragged again podman from brew, and did my testing with podman from brew instead of from the installer.

I'm not sure there's a great solution to this though :-/

### Steps to reproduce the issue

on macOS
- `brew uninstall podman qemu`
- install podman from the installer from podman.io
- install some tool to interact with your containers using brew (eg `mongodb-atlas-cli` following https://www.mongodb.com/docs/atlas/cli/stable/install-atlas-cli/ )
- do not pay attention to the fact that it's pulling podman/qemu from brew as dependencies
- run `podman machine ...`

, while 

### Describe the results you received

This results in podman/qemu being run from brew

### Describe the results you expected

Using the binaries from /opt/podman (the installer) is probably what the user expected.

This is due to brew coming before `/opt/podman/bin` in `$PATH`. brew is added to the path with something like `echo "export PATH=/usr/local/bin:$PATH" >> ~/.bash_profile && source ~/.bash_profile` which puts it first in `$PATH`.
on the contrary, podman installer adds podman to the path by dropping a file in `/etc/paths.d` which is added to the end of `$PATH`.

### podman info output

### Privileged Or Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

Additional environment details

### Additional information

Additional information like issue happens only occasionally or issue happens with a particular architecture or on a particular setting

comments.body: Is there a way to conflict with packages installed from brew?

> Is there a way to conflict with packages installed from brew?

No. I think you would have to modify libpod to only use the first podman binary in $PATH after walking through a predefined set of locations to check first for a podman binary.
There is no libpod?  The user executes podman is going to get the first podman in their path. Podman Desktop could prioritize finding it in the expected path.

It seems to me that mongodb-atlas-cli uses imports from libpod within this repo, but now that look more closely, only for static definitions, but actually calls podman as a separate executable.

I think the easiest (and most immediate fix) may be `brew uninstall --ignore-dependencies podman qemu`, but don't know if  brew would reinstall those when or if updating something that relies on podman like mongodb-atlas-cli.

Duplicate of #20808
Fixed in https://github.com/containers/common/pull/1754 which is in 4.8.2 AFAIK

> Duplicate of #20808 Fixed in [containers/common#1754](https://github.com/containers/common/pull/1754) which is in 4.8.2 AFAIK

This is not a duplicate of #20808.  This issue (#21073) is about podman from brew usually taking precedence over podman from podman.io installer due to the way the `$PATH` env variable is set.

Ah my bad I somehow read qemu.

But if this is about the order on $PATH I am not sure this can be fixed by us in the installer, even if the installer prepends $PATH in bashrc it would still depend on the ordering of the installation if both do `echo "export PATH=/somepath:$PATH" >> ~/.bash_profile` so I don't see how this could be fixed.

Managing $PATH in a sense is always user responsibility, it is impossible to know which version the user prefers.

yeah, not sure at all there's a great solution to this, but filed the issue anyway as the fix for #20808 is only a partial solution when brew can install a podman binary as a dependency of another tool, which will have priority over the already installed podman binary.
podman-machine config could record the full path to the podman binary which was used to create it, and require that this same binary is always used, but this would most likely cause other annoying issues.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
Should this be closed? I do not see how this can be fixed realistically. Managing $PATH will always be user responsibility.
I agree, bottom line is don't use brew for Podman.
